Good afternoon, welcome to the Bryant Park Hotel. Checking in?
Yes, under Ito. I know I'm early -- do you know if the room is ready yet? I'm happy to wait in the lobby if it isn't.
Let me check. Actually, we've moved you to a junior suite on the fourteenth floor -- and yes, housekeeping just finished it. You're all set.
A suite? That's kind of you, thank you. Could you tell me what time checkout is? My flight home is late on Friday.
Checkout is at eleven. If your flight's in the evening, would you like me to note a request for a late checkout? I can't promise it, but I'll flag it.
Please do, thank you. And one more thing -- would you mind adding a wake-up call for six on Monday? I have an early meeting.
Done. Now, I'll just take a deposit of a hundred and fifty dollars -- it's refundable, you get it back at checkout. And I see a valet parking charge on your reservation.
Ah -- I was wondering if you could take that off. There seems to be a valet surcharge, but I didn't bring a car.
You're absolutely right, my apologies -- I'll remove it now. Anything else I can help you with?
That's everything -- you've been very kind. Let me just check I got it all: junior suite, late checkout requested, wake-up call at six Monday, valet charge gone. Is that right?
Would you mind sending someone up to look at it?
I was wondering if you could help me sort out this charge.
There seems to be a surcharge here, and I didn't use the parking.
Could I possibly have a late checkout on Friday?

Good afternoon, Ms. Ito, this is Diane from the front desk at the Bryant Park Hotel, returning your call. I have some news about your stay, and I want to sort a few things out before you arrive. First, the good news. We were not able to hold the standard room you booked, so I have moved you to a junior suite on the fourteenth floor, at no extra charge. It is a bit larger, and it overlooks the park. I hope that works for you. Now, about the two questions you left on your message. You asked whether the room would be ready before three. It will. Housekeeping finishes the upper floors by one, so if you arrive early, just come to the desk and we will check you in. You also asked about the parking surcharge on your confirmation. That is a valet fee of forty-two dollars a night, and I completely understand why you would want to remove it, because you are not bringing a car. I have already taken it off. One last thing. I saw the note that you would like a late checkout on your last day. I cannot promise it yet, but if you remind us the night before, we will do everything we can. Please call back if there is anything else. We look forward to having you with us.

Welcome back to The Careful Traveler. Today, one small idea that has saved me more money and more bad nights than anything else: how you complain at a hotel decides what you get. Here is the thing nobody tells you. The person at the front desk can do almost anything for you, and they will do it for the guest who is calm, and they will not do it for the guest who is loud. Same problem, same desk, two completely different results. So here is my rule. Never start with the problem. Start with a question. Instead of, this room is filthy, try, would you mind sending someone up to take another look at the room? Instead of, I want a refund, try, I was wondering if you could help me sort out the charge on my bill. You are saying the exact same thing, but now you are on the same side of the desk. And one more, because this is the one people get wrong. When you ask an indirect question, keep the words in the normal order. It is, do you know if the pool is open, not, do you know is the pool open. It sounds small. It is not. The soft version is the one that gets the upgrade, gets the late checkout, gets the surcharge removed. Be kind, be specific, and ask like you expect a yes.
